comments.title: Magic and Hormones
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Are there magical ways to influence hormone levels? I'm approaching 50 and want to keep my testosterone levels as high as possible to support my bodybuilding, powerlifting, and martial arts. I don't want to go on Testosterone Replacement Therapy, as I think that our hormones are a symphony and artificially boosting one of them this way would create imbalances. I'm doing all I can with nutrition and behavior - and working on improving sleep and stress management. But, I'm wondering if there are magical approaches I could add in. I don't know much about the berserkers, but it seems like they could be an example of tapping into something magical to increase characteristics generally associated with high testosterone. Thanks!

replies.content:
Well, what kind of magic do you know how to do?

Generally speaking, occult tradition includes ways to work with the endocrine glands, but those have to be handled very carefully -- you can end up stone cold dead if you mess up badly enough. As for the berserkers, yes, they had high testosterone, but they also had sudden fits of homicidal mania -- I'm not sure if that's something you really want to cultivate these days.

I’ve recently fallen down a weird rabbit hole, because I found two things that might tie together in a very strange way.

1) A lot of spiritual practices lower insulin. If you’re interested, I can go through the physiology, but among others, waking before dawn, fasting, vegetarian diets, avoiding sugar, minimizing alcohol, relaxation, hard physical labor, and nicotine and caffeine (common in sacred plants) all lower insulin. All of these are common in spiritual traditions (especially the more intense forms in places such as monasteries), which suggest that there might be a link between low insulin and spirituality.

2) A lot of things common in modern environments raise insulin. Things such as the lack of sidewalks making it hard to walk around; light pollution; the efforts to avoid salt; restrictions on tobacco; the widespread use of marijuana; the common use of all sorts of medicines which damage liver function; the effort from so many to avoid sunlight; high quantities of fructose and protein in the diet; among lots of other things. Again, if you’re interested in any of the physiology, I’m happy to explain any of these.

At this point, I’ve come up with a hypothesis based on these two points. As a result of this toxic stew, insulin levels for most people today (even healthy people) in wealthy nations are at levels at least more than double those of people living traditional lifestyles. This suggest a possible explanation for one of the more striking aspects of modernity, namely that a lot of people find it difficult or impossible to have any spiritual experiences.

This hypothesis would also fit other details rather well: a lot of the things which raise insulin became a lot worse in the late 1970s and early 1980s, are much worse in the US than elsewhere, and the whole toxic stew really first started to take shape in the early 20th century, it suggests a link between modernity and excess insulin, mediated by insulin appearing to reduce spiritual experiences.

a) Does this analysis sound plausible to you?

b) Can you think of any way to test this? I do plan on continuing to track my spiritual practices and seeing if they go into overdrive as I force my insulin levels down as part of my effort to improve my health, but this is not an ideal test. The problem I see with this is that part of how I am going about it it involves implementing a lot of practices known to improve spirituality; so it's not exactly a fair test.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, no, and sometimes?

Diabetes, like so many other chronic illnesses, is defined by a set of symptoms and measurements, rather than by a mechanism or causal factor. The metabolic chain is very very complex, and there are any number of things that can and do go wrong, anywhere along that chain, where the end result is a set of test readings that equal "diabetes".

If you really wanna deep-dive down that rabbit hole, there's a bunch of fascinating literature out there on T1, T2, LADA, MODY (in all its variants), and the various heritable and acquired lipodystrophies. Type 2 is where all the squish is: I don't think we have a good grasp on what that even is, what causes it, or if there's one cause or a whole lot of causes. Would not shock me if etheric starvation was one of several things playing into it.

What's funny to me is, the more I've learned about it, the more I incline to an occult explanation for the thing-- my family appears to have one of the MODY variants (autosomal dominant genetic mutation). But the thing is, the more I look at it, trace it down the family line... the less it looks like a medical condition, and the more I think back on Old Testament references to various offenses leading families to be cursed "to the seventh generation". We're six generations in, and part of me really, really wonders what dreadful thing my great-great-great grandparents got us into!

replies.content:

There's a file-clerk part of my brain that wants desperately to tuck all these things into neat little boxes labeled "health" and "religion" and they just don't sort out in any kind of a tidy way. Anecdotally though: yeah, insulin levels have an outsized influence on spiritual perception, ability to pray, states of consciousness, etc, and the relationship seems to be inverse: high insulin levels equal dampened perception.

b) I can think of several attack points to track this myself, but they all involve idiosyncratic perceptual issues and are not broadly applicable. A home glucometer and a notebook would be an excellent starting point: make notes on your current state, note time, note glucose number, keep a running log? Do keep in mind that the correlation between *insulin levels* and blood glucose levels is not one-to-one. Your liver has a lot to say in the matter, and sometimes it meddles in ways that don't make intuitive sense. If you want to add to your dataset, ketone/urine test strips are pretty cheap, and would give you a clearer picture of what's going on metabolically.

*If you do run experiments on this, I'd be delighted to see an update once you've got some data collected.

c) Not a question you or anybody I think can answer at this point, but for some time I've been extremely curious about the implications of the insulin/perception/consciousness thing, and the drastic increase in T2 diabetes, and rise in the use of exogenous insulin, as well as "insulin sensitizing" drugs. replies.title: Protein?
replies.content: I was under the impression that a high protein diet helped reduce insulin - am I wrong? Isn't that one of the reasons you see so many people on the carnivore diet these days? Also, what does an ideal low-insulin diet look like? Lots of vegetables, pulses, and grains?

replies.title: Re: Protein?
replies.content:
Not the OP, and definitely not an expert, but the ketogenic diet was originally developed as a treatment for diabetes before the ready availability of injectable insulin. If you eat enough protein, your body breaks it down into glucose, which triggers insulin release, though not as quickly or readily as eating starches does. Ketogenic diets are high in fat, as your body turns to breaking fats down into ketones in the absence of glucose (fasting has the same effect, and many people seeking to get into ketosis fast for a day or two to kickstart the process). Ketones can pass the blood-brain barrier, which regular fatty acids cannot. My understanding is that the carnivore diet can help with insulin levels by way of getting a less insulinogenic ratio of fats to proteins, along with a lack of starches, rather than the high protein itself causing that. As always, not medical advice, which diets work best for different folks are highly variable, and so forth.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Protein?
replies.content:
There are multiple ways to reduce insulin. High protein isn't necessarily the most effective: if you eat protein in excess of your body's needs, your body converts the rest into glycogen and stores it in your liver for later. Excess protein can raise your blood sugar, and elevated blood sugar will in turn raise insulin (assuming a functional metabolism).

The most direct route to lower insulin is simply fasting: don't eat anything, insulin levels tend to go down.

Also effective is *any* hypocaloric diet: i.e. whatever you are eating, it's less calories than you are using and your body has to go fish around in your fat/tissue reserves to make up the difference. In order to release energy stores from cells, your body has to drop insulin levels.

This is basically what happens with monks (across religions): largely vegan, but also generally following dietary guidelines that have them eating *just* enough food to get by, punctuated by periods of not eating anything at all.

In the weight-loss diet scene, hypocaloric veganism obviously less popular, and some of the workarounds people use to get to the same lower-insulin place are, diets where most calories come from (mostly saturated) fats, which sort of bypass the need for insulin in the metabolic chain, and various forms of intermittent fasting. It's still really really easy to boot yourself out of the low insulin target area by eating too much protein. And for some of us who are metabolic cripples, there are some less-predictable complications, like exercise raising blood sugar (and thus insulin), dawn phenomenon (blood sugar drops overnight, your liver freaks out and dumps a bunch of cortisol and glycogen into your blood stream, and next thing you know, high blood sugar followed by high insulin, at 3am, and you wake up in a puddle of sweat from a horrific dream).

That said, if you're a metabolically normal person, and you switch from a high junk food high sugar diet, to a high protein diet, odds are good your insulin will go down some ;)

replies.title: Re: Protein?
replies.content:
OP here: based on what I've read, I'll try to answer these questions.

a) Some amount of protein is needed for survival and building new tissues. Past that amount though, the body converts some of the excess protein into glucose (blood sugar). This is normally not a problem though: if it would push blood sugar levels up too high, it can also just be excreted in urine.

These effects are comparatively small, and given that a lot of people have issues digesting most of the best non-meat based sources of protein, it's not really worth worrying about for most people. If your goal is to get insulin down into the normal range, then a diet with lots of meats in it is fine. If your goal is to get your insulin levels down to supra-physiological levels, then meat becomes a problem.

b) The carnivore diet is a huge step up from the standard American diet, but I suspect that it's also a reaction against the more militant and aggressive kind of vegan, and not being done entirely for the health benefits.

c) How low do you want to go? For most people, cutting out alcohol, sugar, seed oils, and refined carbs (especially whatever the US does white flour) will go a long way towards cutting insulin levels down, and if followed for a while with relaxation and the like to address other issues that push insulin up, will probably get most people's insulin levels down to the normal range for our species.

Timing is also important, and so I'd suggesting eating no more than three meals a day; but plenty of people get by on fewer, and if your goal is to crash your insulin levels, fewer is better. This is why a lot of people are doing the one meal a day thing, and it does appear to work.

For a diet that generates supra-physiological insulin levels, you could look into the diet prescribed by The Rule of Saint Benedict: no more than two meals a day, consisting of some rye bread, legumes, vegetables, porridge, and a modest amount of fruit from the garden, fish, poultry, or diluted wine for some meals. Wednesdays and Fridays were only one meal all year; in winter and lent, only one meal per day.

There are others, but that's the one I know the most about, and it was the one that made me really start noticing there was something weird going on here.

replies.title: Diet, digestion, experience, alchemy
replies.content:
It makes sense to try to focus on a single variable (e.g., insulin), but doing so may be quite an over-simplification. There's quite a body of esoteric tradition about organ systems, diet, and so on, and it's not clear how to bring this lore to bear on more current medical models.

One example of this is the suggestion by Joseph Needham that Chinese alchemical recipes involving the processing of urine from boys could be understood as referring to extraction of hormones from urine. Whether or not that suggestion holds up, it's clear that no one could have interpreted Chinese alchemical processes in that way without a prior idea about the possibility of extracting hormones from urine.

Closer to home, Paul Foster Case taught that alchemical transformations leading to long life (etc.) were grounded in the intestines, and that access to these transformative processes could be obtained my meditation on certain Tarot cards. Decades ago, these ideas were sometimes treated with irreverence by whippersnappers who were amused by the colonic obsessions of certain health movements (no pun intended). At that time, however, the complexities of the gut microbiomes had barely begun to be investigated, and the whole issue could probably be re-considered in terms of current ideas about gut-brain interactions, immunity, and so on.

Again, Dion Fortune makes the point, in Psychic Self-Defense, that some forms of psychic attack are facilitated by something like digestive malfunctions: a point perhaps too easily dismissed as an outmoded and cranky obsession with intestinal processes. Perhaps there is more to it than that.

Similarly, the multiplex medicinal brews of traditional medicines have been ransacked for "active ingredients" that could be interpreted by the current physiological models (as in the isolation of digitalis) -- assuming that, once confounding factors were cleared away, there would be some straightforward relationship between the active ingredient and the physiological effect. But the rationale behind this approach does not take into account the possibility of complex, multi-system effects -- on the gut, the heart, and so on -- that the current models can barely begin to recognize.

Now, of course, one can only investigate in accord with what one understands to be the case. But, given the uncertainties of current medical models, it's important to be open to the likelihood that a lot is going on that is not captured by current models. As a simple example, insulin levels may play a role in shifts in consciousness -- but what about variations in light levels? What about protracted periods of what might be called "tonal breath control"? What about pheromonal activation by scents or tastes?-- These may look like single variables that could be controlled, but their target systems (so to speak) might not be so easily factored out.

There's a great deal in the deposit of the esoteric traditions that we simply cannot yet recognize. By recognizing that, however, we might begin to be able to get a better handle on what is there.

LeGrand

Dion Fortune once opined something related to soil science (this is a quote from "Psychic Self-Defense" tangential to a discussion of the protective effect of running water; there might be a more direct one somewhere else):

> We have very little exact knowledge concerning these subtle forces which are the basis of both occult attack and spiritual healing, but we have good reason to believe that in their nature they are closely analogous to electricity. They are not inanimate forces, however, but have in their nature something that is akin to life, though of a low type. It has been my experience that if we work on a blended analogy of electricity and bacteriology, we get pretty near the facts; as near, at any rate, as our present state of knowledge permits. In other words, if we act as if thought possessed the combined qualities of electricity and bacteria we shall have a sufficiently accurate method of steering by dead reckoning in the absence of certain knowledge and actual sight. If we consider the various methods used in folk-magic of all ages and races, we shall observe that they are in agreement with these hypotheses. Running water, we know, has peculiar electrical qualities [...]

She was trying to use generic terms, but with more scientific background it's possible to speculate with slightly more precise choices of generalizations. "Electricity" can be rendered as "substances with charges which move and equilibrate under forces governed by parabolic partial differential equations, as well as substances constituted by propagating excitations within those equations themselves", and "bacteriology" can be rendered as "things analogous to microbial communities".

Also, the laws governing balanced sublimation and impactation exchanges look slightly analogous to the redox chemistry governing a lot of microbial productivity, but maybe that's a coincidence.

replies.title: Imagination and Will
replies.content:
Hi Mr. Greer,

Not the OP here; but thanks for your insight into the mechanics of magic.

I was going to ask a question about the fundamentals, of Imagination and Will. I am sure that, as a non-magician, I have my notions about these concepts wrong. But as a Gurdjieffian who practices internal alchemy, I have always been wary about developing these two qualities, as they are popularly thought of.

Imagination is frowned on in Gurdjieffianism because the goal here is the attainment of “objective Consciousness”, or looking at or seeing Reality objectively.

Will is a funny term, but I have always shied away from the idea of developing “will power”, because a. it seems to preclude the “will of the Divine”, and b. it doesn’t innately include consideration of other people’s needs or will. But the Fourth Way centralizes a nascent type of will called “friction”, where a person develops his/her internal essence through struggling in daily life to take the better internal path. Eventually, Will is developed, but in a spiritual context.

The result of the preceding two approaches is a sensitivity and awareness of the mental structures of those around one, and an ability to navigate the mental world on a personal and macrocosmic level, for the benefit of others.

The unfortunate fact is that there are very few Gurdjieffians who approach the Work on an internal level, in any systematic way.

My guess, is that a magician taking the path of “high magic” eventually achieves a will and imagination of a spiritual nature?

replies.title: Re: Imagination and Will
replies.content:
This is one of those cases -- and of course there are many of them -- in which it's crucial to remember that different paths have their own requirements. I haven't studied or practiced the Fourth Way work -- I've read a few books, but that's all. Thus I'm not sure to what extent we can even speak a common language here.

In occult teachings there are two wills, lower and higher, and most people haven't developed either. (Most people are motivated by desire, not will.) The occult path involves educating desire, developing the lower will, and then aligning both of them with the higher will, which is the will of the Divine as it is expressed in the highest levels of the self.

In occult teachings, the imagination is much more than just fantasy. It's through imagination that we assemble the disconnected flurry of sensory stimuli into a coherent world. From an occult perspective, objective consciousness is impossible -- for consciousness to exist at all, there must be a perceiving subject as well as a perceived object, and if you have a subject, you have subjective consciousness. (At the highest levels of attainment, consciousness is neither objective nor subjective -- it's the matrix from which subject and object emerge.) So you develop imagination in order to bring under conscious control the process by which you create your subjective world, and then use that control first to solve your problems, and then to render the subjective world transparent to the Divine.

That is to say, it's a different path from the Fourth Way -- but then there are many valid paths, of course.

replies.title: Re: Imagination and Will
replies.content:
Most people don't actually know what they desire. They only know what they think they desire, and that's why so often, when they get what they think they want, they end up disappointed and disillusioned. By a process of sustained reflection and exploration, it's possible to get past the programming we all receive from our families, our teachers, and (above all else) the media, and realize what you really want -- and once you do that, getting it, and achieving genuine success and happiness, is not far to seek.

Plato wrote about this in his dialogue The Symposium. It's been a core concept in Western esoteric spirituality ever since.

Thanks, but I'm actually not too concerned about concealing the date itself but the information that can be gleaned from the date line. I personally like "Monday, 10. October 2025 [line break] 20:46, [place]". Even with concealed numbers, that's a fairly obvious structure.

Suppose then that, as an example, I'm using the Vigenère cipher (this problem applies to all easily useable ciphers, except posssibly one time pads, depending on your definition of "easily") with the key "OCCULTISM". That gives "YMLJPF, 10. GKHAZCX 2025 [line break] 20:46 [ESSKS]."

As every day ends in "DAY", you can tell that it's a Vigenère cipher (or something similar) because the letters are shifted differently based on their position in the word. That already lets you figure out all but the first three letters of the key, though you of course don't know the real length. The first three are reversible because you know that "WEDNESDAY" is the only day with nine letters. At this point, you can guess that you've got the correct key but even if that doesn't work out in a scenario with a longer key, it's just a matter of trying different key lengths until the result looks reasonable and reversing the remaining letters.

comments.title: PGM
comments.content:
John Michael Green, can we talk about how the ancient Greek Magical Papyri stacks up against the Hermetic Kabbalah practices by the Golden Dawn? Both traditions are still widely practiced today, yet they don’t seem to overlap at all, which is kind of strange. People who work with the PGM often swear by its ability to deliver real, life-changing results, while Golden Dawn practitioners tend to stay tight-lipped and rarely share anything about their work. PGM users also often say that not relying on Kabbalistic angels is actually a strength—claiming that angels are slower and less effective when it comes to getting things done in the material world. They argue that the spirits in the PGM are far more powerful and that the spells are already finely tuned to achieve specific goals, whereas Golden Dawn angel work might not even be practical in a real emergency.

In fact, some wonder whether the Golden Dawn system is even built for practical results at all. It often demands years of foundational training, complex initiations, and layered symbolism just to perform a single ritual with tangible intent—assuming one ever gets that far. Of course to the outsider, it would all seem pointless. Something like this occured to Lon Milo Duquette who in a fit of impulsiveness called out a demon for a much needed relief from financial stress and thankfully survived the ordeal.


By contrast, the PGM offers invocations like the Stele of Jeu, a self-contained rite that aligns the practitioner directly with an overwhelmingly powerful divine presence, granting immediate spiritual authority to command lesser spirits in a priestly manner. There’s no lengthy hierarchy to climb, no grades to ascend—just a direct line to power. Of course only after considerable practice.

Moreover, the PGM is remarkably adaptable; its frameworks can be reworked to fit nearly any cultural context, personal need, or magical objective without losing their core efficacy. This flexibility, combined with its immediacy, makes the PGM feel strikingly accessible compared to the Golden Dawn’s rigid and time-intensive structure.

replies.title: Re: PGM
replies.content:
There are many different kinds of magic, and it's quite common for people who've only studied one to badmouth the others. I've worked with both those systems, as it happens, as well as several others. I find GD magic extremely effective in practical workings -- anyone who thinks that GD practitioners only work with Cabalistic angels doesn't know that much about the system -- and the fact that GD practitioners don't get into online pissing contests with practitioners of other systems strikes me as a virtue, not a vice.

Did you ever read Norse mythology? There's this nasty little squirrel named Ratatosk who runs up and down the world tree, carrying gossip from each of the Nine Worlds to the others, and trying to start fights. I don't recommend imitating him.

replies.title: Re: PGM
replies.content:
Not the OP but I totally disagree with the OP there.

The idea of working with kabbalistic angels through the Golden Dawn system sounds extremely attractive to me. Why? Hmm it's like you said. Don't imitate the squirrel.

1. Angels are mature, intrepid, powerful beings that have achieved wonders for the world, who don't engage in gossip, or demand an entire life of worship. They may be slow or quick to respond which is why people mistakenly claim that angels aren't effective or propitious to the invoker but I've also been told that angelic workings grow stronger with the magician.

2. Not being the same as gods, they're the easiest entities to work with which is probably why the Golden Dawn, Martinism and Rosicrucian traditions will never stop working with them.

3. Weirdos like demonologists stay away from them. Angel workers tend to be quiet and composed at least those who are effective in their workings.

4. Plethora of ancient and modern spirit work is based on angels, the best is probably the Enochian system of John Dee.

5. But then again the criticism was levied against Golden Dawn, not kabbalistic angels. Ok granted that a well put traditional golden dawn ceremony may take 'hours' but that's for a reason and once someone gets the habit of doing it they finally realise why those steps were placed therein.
You can tell that if there were any indications towards golden dawn spirits rejecting the methods, by god some scrying activity would have revealed so. Only their astrology was basic and subpar compared to the Renaissance astrology.

6. Elements and Zodiac. This is where they often smirk. The 4 elemental theory is not found in the grimoires the way it was taught in the order. Eliphas Levi never provided the source of the 4 kings he wrote about. Agrippa never wrote about Undines, gnomes, salamanders and sylphs and the zodiac never featured in grimoires as the planets received all the attention.

Golden Dawn having Kabbalistic (ballistically powerful) angels solved this problem. There are assigned entities for all forces of nature, the 4 elemental archangels (if you don't jive with the 'fake' Kings), the 7 planetary archangels and intelligences, the 7 Olympic spirits, and the 12 zodiacal archangels also from various grimoires, the 72 angels of Shem Hamephorash, the entire Enochian Corpus. On the surface it looks like it's all angelic work but newsflash, the order emphasized non-demonic workings which just happens to be the celestial realm.
Angel magick is about as safe as it gets within reason and it's modern incarnations continue to publish new materials on its development.

replies.title: Re: PGM
replies.content:
I've done a lot of angel magic, as it happens -- when I was working with the Golden Dawn system, that was a major focus of mine, and now that I'm doing a lot of Martinist work I've taken up angel magic again with equally good results. I don't use angelic workings to try to mooch favors or get stuff -- I don't need that, other forms of magic that don't require hassling spiritual beings are at least as effective if I need to make changes in my life or circumstances -- but to commune with beings that are more intelligent than we are, to learn, and to direct blessing and healing to those who are in need? Angel magic's excellent for that.

One great problem with a lot of the currently fashionable magical systems is that so many of their practitioners are fundamentalists. You know how Christian fundamentalists like to say, "God said it, I believe it, and that settles it"? In this case it's "The PGM said it," or "This grimoire said it," or what have you. It's the same rigid attitude, backed by the same craving to be better than other people. They'd be more effective mages if they got over themselves.

replies.title: Re: PGM
replies.content:
Could you mention some of the other forms of magic that don't require hassling spiritual beings to make changes in life or circumstances? Its always been whats attracted me to magic, but so many systems I've come across have a large effort put into working with spirits. I always liked what I read about the GD in terms of self development, but again it seemed to have a large spirit-work component. Maybe I'm wrong here. Can a person practice the GD system without much in the way of conjuration?

Are there simpler systems that a person can develop their self-magical power to make the changes to life/circumstance that don't rely on calling spirits?

replies.title: Re: PGM
replies.content:
Most systems of magic don't rely on conjuration; we're just going through a period in which demonolatry is fashionable. Most forms of folk magic, including hoodoo and European folk magic, don't do much with spirits. The systems of magic I learned from my teacher John Gilbert, which you can find in books of mine such as The Druid Magic Handbook and The Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose, doesn't use evocation at all. As for the Golden Dawn, evoking spirits is only one narrow branch of the lore -- it doesn't come into the work at all until you've mastered the Z-2 manuscript, which is very advanced stuff.

So, yes, there are many such options. Some of the simplest don't even use magic as such, and focus purely on developing will and imagination in everyday life. A study course along these lines that helped me hugely is available for free download here:

https://iapsop.com/archive/materials/wing_lessons/order_of_the_essenes_florida/

The lessons are meant to be done one per week. The first course of 23 lessons is the most important.

replies.title: Re: PGM
replies.content:
Depending on what you count as magic, any New Thought system will do this, and they rely only on your own internal powers. In addition to the one JMG posted (The Order of Essenes), I'd also heartily recommend The Strangest Secret by Earl Nightengale. I also know people who claim to have had good luck with It Works! by RHJ.

An early edition of the latter is out of copyright and can be found here:

https://coolwisdombooks.com/it-works-by-r-h-j-1926/

For The Strangest Secret, the publisher offers a free transcript of the original audio recording on their website here:

https://www.nightingale.com/articles/the-strangest-secret/

Both of these are results-oriented without promising the moon (though a few passages in It Works! meander towards that territory).

replies.title: Re: PGM
replies.content: Oh, there are plenty of other systems out there. I like to recommend the Essenes course because I worked through it, and it turned my life around -- that was what taught me just how many of my problems were my own doing, and showed me how to make my career take off like a rocket. Which it then did!

replies.title: Re: PGM
replies.content:
That's certainly high praise for the system, and great to hear.

As for my reply, I hope I didn't come across as trying to upstage you; I've just been thinking for a while about what I would recommend to someone starting out and those two always stand out for me. So just think of it as me getting a chance to do a mini nerd-out.

One of these days I'm going to have to nerd out about Neville Goddard, and how to read him profitably without having a psychotic break.

replies.title: Re: PGM
replies.content: I've been doing magic for the greater part of my 83 years, and never once have I had reason to evoke any spirit.  (My ancestors are always a part of me, and they do not need to be evoked.)

replies.title: Re: PGM
replies.content: The ancient texts published in PGM are not part of a single unified system, but were written in several different centuries and at many different places along the Nile, by many and various magicians. Imagine, for comparison, someone a thousand years in the future has discovered several fragmentary GD flying rolls, a booklet by William Walker Atkinson, a fragment of Stephen E. Flower's excellent book Runes and Magic, and Lady Sheba's Grimoire, and has published them together under the illusion that they are parts of a single coherent American magical system. The only difference would be that the scholarly editors of the PGM were under no such illusion.

comments.title: Visualization exercise
comments.content:
I have a visualization exercise that I use when I need to get in the right mood to handle something difficult or stressful. I find it very effective and thought I would share it both for others to use and to get any commentary or suggestions from our host. It goes as follows:

1. You are standing in a pool of water in a tropical forest. A waterfall is pouring into the pool in front of you. You step into the waterfall and feel your emotions being washed away.

2. You climb up into the mountains and find yourself standing on above the clouds on a dark peak. Moonlight shines down on you and a cool wind blows over you, clearing your mind and refreshing your body.

3. You climb down into a valley where you find small river of lava flowing. The radiating heat from the lava fills the air, surrounding, warming and energizing you.

4. You climb into a cave. It is cool and slightly damp inside. You lay down and feel the earth calming you and pulling away and excess energies to leave you feeling balanced and ready for whatever you need to do.

comments.title: Buying Stability after an unstable year
comments.content:
Hi JMG
I've had a difficult year: I was the main caregiver for my father who died in July; then me and my GF parted ways because she wanted to have kids and I did not. So I'm left without anything "solid", I'm a state of transhumance. My father left me some capital and I'm trying to buy a house so that I'll have at least something stable in my life, but I'm awful at these practical things, I'm one of those easy-to-fool intellectual types, and I'm having a really hard time resolving this.

So my question is twofold:
- Is there anything I can do to be less fearful of realtors/property owners, to sort of protect myself from the massive distrust I have in them?
- There is one property I really like but it'll take a few months until some ancient levies are lifted and the property can be properly sold, but I fear the seller might get cold feet or change their mind in the meantime. I can't make them sign a binding contract because here in Chile those are discouraged when a property has this sort of levies. So I want to sort of "bind" the property, or I don't know how to put it, but I want to have more power or control over the outcome of this transaction. No can do through legal means, so it must be some sort of magic I guess.

My knowledge of magic is limited to reading your posts and listening to your interviews. I have zero practical knowledge.

Thanks in advance.

replies.title: Re: Buying Stability after an unstable year
replies.content:
1) Well, are you sure the fear is inappropriate? I have no idea what things are like in Chile, but up here in the US, a lot of real estate agents are dishonest and will cheat you if they have the chance; a lot of property owners, for that matter, are trying to get the best deal they can and aren't necessarily ethical about it.

2) Unfortunately, if you don't have any practical knowledge of magic, trying to turn to magic to fix things generally won't work. It's something that takes plenty of knowledge and practice to get results.

3) I'm going to offer a piece of unsolicited advice, which you're certainly free to ignore. Jumping into a house purchase or, really, anything else when you're still reeling from a difficult time is unlikely to turn out well. When my wife died a year and a half ago, the best advice I got -- and it came from several people who'd all been through similar situations -- was to make no important decisions and avoid any significant change for at least a year. That kept me from doing some very stupid things. I know the feeling of transhumance is awkward, but I'd encourage you to consider living with it for a while, until you've worked through your emotions and are in a better space to make important decisions.

replies.title: Re: Buying Stability after an unstable year
replies.content: Thank you very much for your response. The advice is really welcome.

replies.title: Re: Buying Stability after an unstable year
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Buying Stability after an unstable year
replies.content:
You might want to reread what I wrote -- "make no important changes." Stopping work on a system of magic in mid-course is an important change; my advice is to make no important changes, i.e., keep working.

Thus, when my wife died early in 2024, I kept up my practices. It was the right decision.

comments.title: Rubber Band Effect
comments.content:
Greetings to all -

I'm curious what advice you would give about ways to mitigate what I've heard called the "rubber band effect": that phenomenon that happens when you begin a new practice, and then notice that as soon as it becomes apparent that you're serious, everything in your orbit (including, in some ways, your own self) goes to war with you to prevent any meaningful progress.

Like running away from a wall you're attached to by a rubber band, and getting snapped back - with all the associated pain, frustration, and negativity.

I'm not trying anything terribly complicated, just basic centering and protective prayer, meditation, and very light ritual. After about a week and a half of this, I'm finding myself overwhelmed by distractions and... temptations, for lack of a better word, at a level of intensity I haven't experienced for years.

I'm aware that I do not have a great strength of will, largely due to having enough natural aptitude for enough things that I wasn't forced to strengthen it during my earlier years. I'm also a novice at intentional spiritual practice, and for various reasons I'm not currently able to access like-minded support in person. I'd be grateful for the wisdom of this community.

replies.title: Re: Rubber Band Effect
replies.content: In traditional occult lore that's called karmic culmination. What's happening is that you're being brought face to face with your unfinished business. I know, it sucks, but if you grit your teeth and keep going, you'll push through it in time, and then a lot of problems that have hindered you in your life will do away.

replies.title: Re: Rubber Band Effect
replies.content: Would this be basically the same things as encountering "the watcher at the threshhold" or is that different?

replies.title: Re: Rubber Band Effect
replies.content: The term "the watcher at the threshold" has been used by different teachers and traditions to mean different things. Some use it to mean the same thing as karmic culmination, others use it for a difficulty later on -- when you first get to the point of being able to glimpse your own higher self, quite often those first glimpses terrify the lower self and it imagines a vast lurking monster, or what have you.

comments.title: Alan Leo on Uranus & Neptune
comments.content:
A comment on last week's MM got me to skim Alan Leo's Esoteric Astrology to find the age of the soul. (There's a lot to his book's system, but the soul-age seems to be about how the soul comes to rule his houses, then the signs, and finally the planets themselves.) The author claimed that most people are not advanced enough to respond to even the lower vibrations of Uranus. I remembered him making similar claims about Neptune in The Key to Your Own Nativity (I thought he was just hedging his bets because astrologers of his time probably hadn't fully worked out what Neptune does. That doesn't work for Uranus, though-- its malefic influence was well known by the 1830s).

As far as I know from the Internet, most people are influenced by Uranus and Neptune, including those who are heavily influenced by their houses. So why would Alan Leo make those claims?

1. He's actually correct, and most people aren't influenced by the ice giants, but modern natal astrologers assume they are and find observations which they could construe as the influences of Uranus and Neptune. And younger souls are less likely to get into astrology beyond the pop culture Sun sign level.

2. Alan Leo was dogmatically biased and ignored the data that showed that most people were influenced by those planets.

3. I am misunderstanding what the author is really saying, since I didn't really study the book.

4. Uranus & Neptune behaved like that in his time (or shortly before it), but over the 20th century, their influence in natal charts and transits has extended to most people.

Patrick

replies.title: Re: Alan Leo on Uranus & Neptune
replies.content: My guess is that it's a mix of #2 and #4. Leo had his biases, as we all do, and of course you're right that the two outermost planets weren't yet well understood in his time; at the same time, the process by which a genuinely new astrological factor expresses itself in human life is complex, and not always fast.

comments.title: Templars and Sabians of Harran
comments.content: Hi JMG. Do you think the Knights Templar received the esoteric teachings from Sabians of Harran? If not, what alternative society is the most likely source?

replies.title: Re: Templars and Sabians of Harran
replies.content: It's impossible to say. In the 12th century, when the Templars were active, the Middle East was still full of little esoterically inclined sects, many of which were Gnostic Christians of various kinds. It seems very likely that the Templars contacted at least one of these, and maybe more than one -- under the circumstances, they probably went out of their way to recruit members of these little sects as native auxiliaries, since they had been persecuted by the Muslims and so would be loyal to the Crusaders. But I know of no hard evidence that would allow a specific source to be identified.

replies.title: Re: Templars and Sabians of Harran
replies.content: Along with Gnostic Christians, it is also often speculated that the Templars were in contact with the Order of Assassins (Hashshashiyun) who were steeped in Sufi mysticism and magic.

replies.title: Re: Templars and Sabians of Harran
replies.content: I think there's at least some documentary evidence supporting this. The Templars were a major power in the Middle East during the heyday of the Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem, and they had their own ambassadors, spies, and contacts all over the region. It's probably more a question about who they weren't in contact with.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No, not at all. Vodou ("voodoo") is a Haitian Afro-diaspora religion mostly derived from West African traditions, especially those of the Fon people in what is now Benin (a major source of slaves for the Caribbean islands). Like most West African traditions, it involves possession trances in which the gods or loa possess their worshippers.

Hoodoo, also known as conjure or rootwork, is a North American Afro-diaspora magical practice -- not a religion -- which derives mostly from the Bakongo peoples of the Congo basin. (Benin is about as far from the Congo as Houston is from Boston -- Africa is big.) Zombis are a Vodou thing, not a hoodoo thing!

comments.title: Hephaestus
comments.content:
Not a question, just something to share. A while back, someone had asked about worshipping Hephaestus. In their most recent newsletter, Greekmyths-interpretation.com did a short write-up on Hephaestus, which I've pasted below. (And for anyone interested in Greek mythology, I highly recommend their website.)

"Hephaestus, skilled blacksmith, son of Zeus and Hera (although according to Hesiod, Hera conceived him alone), is the power to create new, harmonious forms, opposing and complementing the force of destruction embodied by his brother Ares. Hephaestus, crippled and lame, embodies a psychic force still limited in humanity, and his work is primarily aimed at gods, heroes and advanced spiritual seekers.

Twice rejected from Olympus, Hephaestus develops his creative and transformative abilities in secret in the depths of the human vital, hidden from the gaze of the gods, until human consciousness is ready to integrate his constructive force. His first fall is caused by his mother Hera, embarrassed by his infirmity; he is then taken in by Eurynome and Thetis, figures symbolizing respectively divine harmony and inner awareness in the deep vital.

During his second fall caused by Zeus, who was furious at his support of Hera, Hephaestus is saved by the Sintians on the island of Lemnos, representing the union of opposites in man.

Hephaestus' creations, generated with the help of the sacred fire Agni, the flame of aspiration that rises from the soul or psychic being, symbolize yoga practices, realizations and protections accessible only to the purest, freest beings. As a divine craftsman, he forges magical objects for gods and great heroes: weapons, jewels, tripods and automata that symbolize spiritual capacities and tools for inner transformation. He also possessed a talent for architecture, designing celestial dwellings for the gods.

His unfruitful marriage to Aphrodite, goddess of love, suggests that he is working on a form of pure, selfless love that, in the future, can exist without the destruction symbolized by Ares, Aphrodite's lover.

Hephaestus represents the inner strength within us that forges, through divine aspiration (Agni), the spiritual tools enabling us to journey towards unity and transformation of being. He calls on the seeker to harmonize thoughts, emotions and actions under the guidance of the psychic being."

replies.title: Re: Hephaestus

replies.title: Re: Hephaestus
replies.content: Thank you for this.  I will check the site out.  FWIW, the one time I took an oath in the name of a Pagan God and Goddess, the obvious Deities for me to name were Hephaistos (Hephaestus) and Hestia.  These two speak to my heart like few others.
